title: Five Eyes warns of LinkedIn espionage, US delays arms exports to allies, SpaceX breaks ground on domestic chip megafab
subhead: A coordinated intelligence warning from Five Eyes nations named China's recruitment tactics on job platforms. Meanwhile, US operations in the Middle East are reverberating through allied defense programs and East Asian supply chains.

sections.heading: Five Eyes warns of Chinese espionage via job platforms
sections.body:
Security agencies from the Five Eyes alliance — the US, UK, Australia, Canada, and New Zealand — issued an unprecedented joint bulletin warning that Chinese military intelligence is using professional networking sites including LinkedIn, Indeed, and Upwork to recruit Western officials and military personnel. According to AP News and The Guardian, Chinese agents pose as human resources consultants for legitimate-looking companies and post fake job advertisements for positions such as foreign policy or defense analysts.

The bulletin, released Wednesday, describes an 'aggressive' online recruitment strategy. After posting advertisements, agents scrutinize applicants' CVs to identify individuals with security clearances or access to classified information. Successful candidates are asked to write reports on topics such as China's relations with other countries, defense, or trade, then pressured to provide 'non-public' information with payments ranging from a few hundred to several thousand dollars per report via PayPal, Western Union, or cryptocurrency.

Britain's security minister Dan Jarvis urged anyone who could be a potential target to be on guard. The South China Morning Post notes the warning comes despite recent diplomatic efforts to improve ties with Beijing, including US President Trump's meeting with Xi Jinping in May and British Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper's visit to China this week. A Chinese embassy spokesperson called the allegations 'entirely fabricated' and said Five Eyes intelligence gathering was 'the real threat to peace-loving countries.'
sections.whyItMatters: The coordinated nature of the warning — spanning all five alliance members simultaneously — signals an escalation in counter-espionage efforts that could affect how cleared professionals approach job searches and networking. The timing alongside diplomatic overtures to Beijing highlights the growing disconnect between political engagement and intelligence community concerns.

sections.heading: US weapons delays ripple through allied defense programs
sections.body:
Multiple US allies and partners have been told their expected weapons deliveries are being slowed as Washington prioritizes replenishing its own stockpiles after Operation Epic Fury, the campaign against Iran. According to Breaking Defense, the impacts span from Taiwan — where Acting Navy Secretary Hung Cao confirmed a 'pause' on deliveries — to Japan, which faces potential delays in receiving 400 Tomahawk missiles.

A CSIS analysis estimates the US expended 1,000-1,430 Tomahawk missiles and 1,060-1,430 Patriot interceptors during operations over Iran. The think tank warns it could take until 2030-2031 to restore Tomahawk inventories and until mid-2029 for Patriot stockpiles. The administration is pursuing plans to ramp up production of 14 'critical munitions,' but capacity building takes time.

The Financial Times and Reuters report that the UK, Poland, Norway, Estonia, Lithuania, and Switzerland have also been notified of possible delays. Denmark's late-2025 decision to buy French-Italian SAMP/T systems instead of Patriot — cited by CSIS's Tom Karako as driven partly by being 'at the back of a very long line' — illustrates how allied procurement choices may shift. The Trump administration also approved $8.6 billion in emergency arms sales to Israel, Qatar, Kuwait, and the UAE in late May, raising questions about production queue priorities.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran campaign has exposed the strain on US munitions production capacity at a moment when allied reliance on American systems is being tested. As Stimson Center fellow Elias Yousif notes, 'we're only just now starting to see the drivers that may reshape' long-standing patterns of allied dependence on US weapons.

sections.heading: SpaceX wins Texas approval for $55-119B Terafab chip complex
sections.body:
Grimes County commissioners voted 4-1 to approve tax breaks for SpaceX's proposed Terafab semiconductor manufacturing complex in rural Texas, despite fierce opposition from residents concerned about impacts on their community. According to CNBC and the Houston Chronicle, the project — valued at between $55 billion and $119 billion depending on scope — would create approximately 1,800 jobs and bring logic, memory, and advanced packaging under one roof.

SpaceX plans to use Terafab chips for its Starlink satellites and orbital data centers, according to project lead John Federspiel. Elon Musk claims global chipmakers produce only 2% of what Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI will need for electric vehicles, humanoid robots, and space-based computing. The facility would be located near the Gibbons Creek Reservoir outside College Station, using water and infrastructure from a shuttered coal plant.

The timing is notable: SpaceX is preparing what could become the largest IPO in history, with a planned valuation of $1.75 trillion and a share price of $135 to raise $75 billion. Texas officials highlighted the state's existing semiconductor ecosystem — Texas is the No. 1 state for semiconductor exports — while University of Houston semiconductor engineering professor Jae-Hyun Ryou noted that bringing logic, memory, and packaging together 'has never been done' and is 'highly complicated.'
sections.whyItMatters: If built, Terafab would vertically integrate a major portion of SpaceX's semiconductor supply chain, reducing dependency on external foundries for satellite and data center operations. The project also illustrates how the CHIPS Act era is reshaping domestic manufacturing geography beyond traditional hubs like Arizona and Oregon.

sections.heading: China unveils sailless submarine design
sections.body:
Satellite imagery from Vantor (formerly Maxar) captured at Jiangnan Shipyard in Shanghai reveals a new Chinese submarine approximately 394 feet (120 meters) long with a distinctive feature: it lacks a traditional sail — the tower-like structure rising from the hull that houses periscopes, snorkels, and navigation equipment. The War Zone and Naval News first reported on the development.

Undersea warfare analyst H.I. Sutton assessed the design as longer than China's Type 093 nuclear attack submarines (roughly 356-360 feet) and slightly longer than the US Virginia-class SSNs (377 feet). The submarine features an X-form rudder configuration — first seen on Chinese submarines in 2024 — and possibly a shrouded pumpjet propulsor for quieter operation. Jiangnan Shipyard previously launched a smaller sailless technology demonstrator in 2018, suggesting this larger vessel builds on that experience.

The sailless design offers improved streamlining, speed, and reduced acoustic signature, but limits surface navigation and situational awareness. As The War Zone notes, the design may reflect a focus on seabed operations or extended blue-water transits. Meanwhile, Naval News reported another new Chinese submarine — possibly the Type 095 next-generation attack submarine with a traditional sail — was spotted at Bohai Shipyard, indicating parallel development tracks. US Navy Rear Adm. Mike Brookes told the US-China Economic and Security Review Commission in March that China is 'executing a significant strategic shift from diesel-electric to all-nuclear construction.'
sections.whyItMatters: The sailless design, if operational, would represent a significant departure from conventional submarine architecture. Combined with accelerating nuclear submarine production, the development suggests China is investing in new undersea capabilities optimized for extended Pacific operations.

sections.heading: Taiwan accelerates anti-ship missile buildup
sections.body:
Reuters reports Taiwan plans to sharply increase its arsenal of anti-ship missiles to more than 1,800 weapons by early 2029, according to a new report. The expansion aims to create a 'kill zone' in the Taiwan Strait that renders any invasion attempt prohibitively costly. The Hsiung Feng II and III missiles form the core of Taiwan's coastal defense doctrine.

The strategy prioritizes mobile coastal missile batteries rather than attempting to match China ship-for-ship. As WION notes, the goal is to make the island 'too costly to assault' by saturating potential landing zones and denying amphibious approach corridors. Taiwan also signed a new five-year Dassault support contract for its Mirage 2000-5 fighter fleet to maintain availability, AeroTime reported.

In parallel, The Telegraph reported Taiwan is developing armed robot dogs — four-legged machines developed in the US — to patrol remote South China Sea islands where there is 'pressing need' for coastal surveillance. The combination of layered coastal defenses and unmanned systems reflects Taiwan's asymmetric approach to deterring Chinese military action.
sections.whyItMatters: Taiwan's missile buildup represents a deliberate shift toward asymmetric denial capabilities — mobile, survivable systems that complicate Chinese invasion planning without requiring Taiwan to match Beijing's naval construction pace.

sections.heading: Japan and East Asia face naphtha shortage from Hormuz disruption
sections.body:
Japan's plastic bag and packaging industry is facing acute shortages as naphtha — a key petrochemical feedstock extracted from crude oil — becomes scarce due to Middle East disruptions. The Guardian reports that polyethylene production was down 62% in March compared to 2025, according to the Japan Petrochemical Industry Association. Takeaways, supermarkets, and bakeries are running out of bags, trays, and food service gloves.

The Middle East provides most of Japan's crude oil, from which naphtha is derived. The feedstock is used to make printing ink, plastics, adhesives, and medical supplies. Japan's food sector accounts for nearly one-third of annual plastic use exceeding 8 million tonnes. Some retailers are offering perks to customers who bring their own containers; municipalities are allowing residents to use non-approved bags for waste disposal as panic buying depletes supplies.

The impact extends beyond Japan. South Korea saw garbage bag sales jump nearly fivefold in March, prompting purchase restrictions. Taiwan, which imports about 70% of its crude from the Middle East, has seen wholesale plastic prices surge up to 40%, the Straits Times reported. Governments across East Asia are urging citizens to avoid hoarding while monitoring for profiteering.
sections.whyItMatters: The naphtha shortage illustrates how energy and logistics disruptions cascade through industrial supply chains. Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan — all heavily dependent on Middle East crude — are simultaneously exposed to any Hormuz-related disruption, affecting everything from food packaging to medical supplies.

sections.heading: UAE positions as data corridor amid Hormuz cable concerns
sections.body:
Iranian media commentary in recent months has drawn attention to the subsea internet cables passing through the Strait of Hormuz, with some discussing potential fees or geopolitical leverage points. In response, The National (UAE) published an opinion piece arguing the Emirates is 'uniquely positioned' to serve as a digital corridor between the Arabian Sea and Arabian Gulf.

More than 95% of intercontinental data traffic travels through subsea cables. The UAE has direct access to both the Arabian Sea and the Arabian Gulf, enabling cable systems to land on the eastern coast, cross the country via terrestrial fiber infrastructure, and reconnect to networks on the Gulf side — bypassing Hormuz entirely. The country has spent two decades building telecommunications infrastructure, data centers, and cable projects connecting Asia, Africa, Europe, and the Middle East.

The piece frames data security as increasingly parallel to energy security: 'As economies become more digital, these cables are assuming a role not unlike that of energy pipelines in previous decades.' The infrastructure that carries data will become 'every bit as important as the infrastructure that carries energy,' the author argues, positioning the UAE as a trusted connector between East and West.
sections.whyItMatters: The proposal reflects a broader regional recognition that data infrastructure has become strategic terrain. A secure overland cable route through the UAE would reduce Hormuz-related vulnerabilities while cementing the Emirates' role as a digital gateway.

sections.heading: Russia acknowledges oil output decline from Ukrainian drone strikes
sections.body:
Russian Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak publicly acknowledged that Russia's crude oil production has declined since the beginning of 2026 as multiple refineries undergo 'unscheduled repairs.' Speaking at the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum, Novak told reporters that 'Russia's oil production is indeed lower compared to the beginning of 2026.'

According to OilPrice.com, Novak's comments represent the first public admission from Moscow that intensified Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian refining infrastructure are reducing domestic output. Russia has responded by banning jet fuel exports through November 30 and prohibiting gasoline exports since April 1 to ensure domestic supply. Ukraine has also targeted Baltic Sea and Black Sea export terminals, aiming to prevent Russia from benefiting from elevated international prices.

Earlier this week, Ukrainian drones struck an oil terminal in St. Petersburg, setting it ablaze just as the city prepared to host the Russian economic forum. President Zelenskyy stated Ukraine was 'bringing the war back home — to Russia.'
sections.whyItMatters: The acknowledgment marks a shift in Russian official rhetoric about the effectiveness of Ukrainian strikes on energy infrastructure. Combined with export bans, the statement suggests Moscow expects refining disruptions to persist rather than prove temporary.

bottomLine: The Five Eyes warning on Chinese recruitment platforms, combined with cascading effects from US operations in the Middle East on allied weapons programs and East Asian supply chains, underscores how technology and security issues now cut across traditional boundaries. SpaceX's move into domestic chip production represents a new vertical integration model for defense and satellite companies. China's sailless submarine and Taiwan's missile buildup reflect ongoing military-technology competition in the Pacific. The UAE's data corridor proposal highlights how infrastructure competition is expanding from energy to digital networks.
sources: AP News, The Guardian, South China Morning Post, Breaking Defense, CSIS, Stimson Center, Reuters, CNBC, Houston Chronicle, The War Zone, Naval News, OilPrice.com, The National (UAE), WION, AeroTime, The Telegraph, Financial Times
